If a bit is cleared to ‘0’, then the corresponding Read Recovery Level is not supported.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td colspan="2">Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>15</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 15 (RRL15): Fast Fail1</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>14</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 14 (RRL14)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>13</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 13 (RRL13)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>12</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 12 (RRL12)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>11</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 11 (RRL11)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 10 (RRL10)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>9</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 9 (RRL9)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 8 (RRL8)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 7 (RRL7)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 6 (RRL6)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 5 (RRL5)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 4 (RRL4): Default1</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 3 (RRL3)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 2 (RRL2)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 1 (RRL1)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td colspan="2">Read Recovery Level 0 (RRL0)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Notes:1. If Read Recovery Levels are supported, then this bit shall be set to ‘1’.</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="13">102</td><td rowspan="13">M</td><td rowspan="13">R</td><td rowspan="13">R</td><td colspan="2">Boot Partition Capabilities (BPCAP): This field indicates the Boot Partition capabilities supported by the controller.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>07:03</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>02</td><td>Set Features Boot Partition Write Protection Support (SFBPWPS): This bit indicates if Set Features Boot Partition Write Protection is supported by the controller. If supported, this capability allows a host to configure Boot Partition write protection states via the Boot Partition Write Protection Config feature in the Set Features command. Refer to section 8.1.3.3.1.</td></tr><tr><td>Value</td><td>Definition</td></tr><tr><td>0b</td><td>Set Features Boot Partition Write Protection is not supported by this controller.</td></tr><tr><td>1b</td><td>Set Features Boot Partition Write Protection is supported by this controller.</td></tr><tr><td>01:00</td><td>RPMB Boot Partition Write Protection Support (RPMBBPWPS): This field indicates if RPMB Boot Partition Write Protection is supported by the controller. If supported, this capability allows a host to configure Boot Partition write protection states via RPMB. Refer to section 8.1.3.3.2.</td></tr><tr><td>Value</td><td>Definition</td></tr><tr><td>00b</td><td>Support for RPMB Boot Partition Write Protection is not specified. Only controllers compliant with NVM Express Base Specification, Revision 2.0 and earlier are allowed to report this value. Refer to section 8.1.3.3 for more details on when a controller may return this value.</td></tr><tr><td>01b</td><td>RPMB Boot Partition Write Protection is not supported by this controller.</td></tr><tr><td>10b</td><td>RPMB Boot Partition Write Protection is supported by this controller.</td></tr><tr><td>11b</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>103</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>107:104</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">NVM Subsystem Shutdown Latency (NSSL): This field indicates the typical latency in microseconds for an NVM Subsystem Shutdown to complete. Refer to section 3.6.3. A value of 0h indicates that NVM Subsystem Shutdown Latency is not reported.</td></tr><tr><td>109:108</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">110</td><td rowspan="5">O</td><td rowspan="5">O</td><td rowspan="5">R</td><td colspan="2">Power Loss Signaling Information (PLSI): This field indicates information about Power Loss Signaling processing capabilities.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>PLS Forced Quiescence (PLSFQ): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Power Loss Signaling with Forced Quiescence (refer to section 8.2.5.2). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Power Loss Signaling with Forced Quiescence.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>PLS Emergency Power Fail (PLSEPF): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Power Loss Signaling with Emergency Power Fail (refer to section 8.2.5.3). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Power Loss Signaling with Emergency Power Fail.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">111</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td>Controller Type (CNTRLTYPE): This field specifies the controller type. A value of 0h indicates that the controller type is not reported.Implementations compliant with NVM Express Base Specification, Revision 1.4 or later shall report a controller type (i.e., the value 0h is reserved and shall not be used).Implementations compliant with an earlier specification version may report a value of 0h to indicate that a controller type is not reported.</td></tr><tr><td>Value Controller Type</td></tr><tr><td>0h Reserved (controller type not reported)</td></tr><tr><td>1h I/O controller</td></tr><tr><td>2h Discovery controller</td></tr><tr><td>3h Administrative controller</td></tr><tr><td>4h to FFh Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>127:112</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>FRU Globally Unique Identifier (FGUID): This field contains a 128-bit value that is globally unique for a given Field Replaceable Unit (FRU). Refer to the NVM Express® Management Interface Specification for the definition of a FRU. This field remains fixed throughout the life of the FRU. This field shall contain the same value for each controller associated with a given FRU.This field uses the EUI-64 based 16-byte designator format. Bytes 122:120 contain the 24-bit Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI) value assigned by the IEEE Registration Authority. Bytes 127:123 contain an extension identifier assigned by the corresponding organization. Bytes 119:112 contain the vendor specific extension identifier assigned by the corresponding organization. Refer to the IEEE EUI-64 guidelines for more information. This field is big endian (refer to section 4.5.4).When not implemented, this field contains a value of 0h.</td></tr><tr><td>129:128</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Command Retry Delay Time 1 (CRDT1): If the Do Not Retry (DNR) bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27; in the CQE and the Command Retry Delay (CRD) field is set to 01b in the CQE, then this value indicates the command retry delay time in units of 100 milliseconds.</td></tr><tr><td>131:130</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Command Retry Delay Time 2 (CRDT2): If the DNR bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27; in the CQE and the CRD field is set to 10b in the CQE, then this value indicates the command retry delay time in units of 100 milliseconds.</td></tr><tr><td>133:132</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Command Retry Delay Time 3 (CRDT3): If the DNR bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27; in the CQE and CRD field is set to 11b in the CQE, then this value indicates the command retry delay time in units of 100 milliseconds.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">134</td><td rowspan="5">O</td><td rowspan="5">R</td><td rowspan="5">R</td><td>Controller Reachability Capabilities (CRCAP): This field specifies reachability capabilities of the controller and NVM subsystem.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2 Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>1 Reachability Group ID Changeable (RGIDC): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the RGRPID field in the I/O Command Set Independent Identify Namespace data structure (refer to Figure 319) does not change while the namespace is attached to any controller. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the RGRPID field may change while the namespace is attached to any controller. Refer to section 8.1.19.</td></tr><tr><td>0 Reachability Reporting Supported (RRSUP): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem supports Reachability Reporting (refer to section 8.1.19). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem does not support Reachability Reporting.</td></tr><tr><td>239:135</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>252:240</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td>Reserved for the NVMe Management Interface.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">253</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td>NVM Subsystem Report (NVMSR): This field reports information associated with the NVM subsystem. If the controller is compliant with the NVM Express Management Interface Specification, then at least one bit in this field is set to &#x27;1&#x27;. If the NVM subsystem does not support the NVM Express Management Interface Specification, then this field shall be cleared to 0h. Refer to the NVM Express Management Interface Specification.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>NVMe Enclosure (NVMEE): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem is part of an NVMe Enclosure. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem is not part of an NVMe Enclosure.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>NVMe Storage Device (NVMSD): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem is part of an NVMe Storage Device. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem is not part of an NVMe Storage Device.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">254</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td>VPD Write Cycle Information (VWCI): This field indicates information about the remaining number of times that VPD contents are able to be updated using the VPD Write command. Refer to the NVM Express Management Interface Specification for details on VPD contents and the VPD Write command.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>VPD Write Cycles Remaining Valid (VWCRV): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the VPD Write Cycles Remaining field is valid. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the VPD Write Cycles Remaining field is invalid and cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>6:0</td><td>VPD Write Cycles Remaining (VWCR): If the VPD Write Cycle Remaining Valid bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then this field contains a value indicating the remaining number of times that VPD contents are able to be updated in units of 256 bytes using the VPD Write command. For example, a 1 KiB FRU Information Device that can be updated 8 times would indicate a value of 32 in this field. If this field is set to 7Fh, then the remaining number of times that VPD contents are able to be updated using the VPD Write command is greater than or equal to 7Fh. If the VPD Write Cycle Remaining Valid bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then this field is not valid and shall be cleared to a value of 0h.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">255</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td rowspan="5">M</td><td>Management Endpoint Capabilities (MEC): This field indicates the support for Management Endpoints in the NVM subsystem. Refer to the NVM Express Management Interface Specification for details.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>PCIe Port Management Endpoint (PCIEME): If the NVM subsystem contains one or more Management Endpoints on one or more PCIe ports, then this bit shall be set to &#x27;1&#x27;; otherwise, this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>2-Wire Port Management Endpoint (TWPME): If the NVM subsystem contains one or more Management Endpoints on the 2-Wire port, then this bit shall be set to &#x27;1&#x27;; otherwise, this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;. This bit was formerly named SMBus/I2C Port Management Endpoint (SMBUSME).</td></tr><tr><td colspan="5">Admin Command Set Attributes &amp; Optional Controller Capabilities</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3">257:256</td><td rowspan="3">M</td><td rowspan="3">M</td><td rowspan="3">R</td><td>Optional Admin Command Support (OACS): This field indicates the optional Admin commands and features supported by the controller. Refer to section 3.1.3.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>15:12</td><td>Reserved</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="12"></td><td rowspan="12"></td><td rowspan="12"></td><td rowspan="12"></td><td>11</td><td>Host Managed Live Migration Support (HMLMS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Host Managed Live Migration capability (refer to section 8.1.12).If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Host Managed Live Migration capabilitySecondary controllers shall clear this bit to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td>Command and Feature Lockdown Supported (CFLS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Command and Feature Lockdown capability (refer to section 8.1.5). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Command and Feature Lockdown capability. The value of this bit shall be the same for all controllers in the NVM subsystem.</td></tr><tr><td>9</td><td>Get LBA Status Supported (GLSS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Get LBA Status capability with the Action Type values of 10h and 11h (refer to the NVM Command Set Specification). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Get LBA Status capability with the Action Type values of 10h and 11h.</td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td>Doorbell Buffer Config Supported (DBCS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Doorbell Buffer Config command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Doorbell Buffer Config command.</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>Virtualization Management Supported (VMS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Virtualization Management command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Virtualization Management command.</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>NVMe-MI Send Receive Supported (NSRS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVMe-MI Send and NVMe-MI Receive commands. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the NVMe-MI Send and NVMe-MI Receive commands.</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>Directives Supported (DIRS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Directives. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Directives. A controller that supports Directives shall support the Directive Send and DirectiveReceive commands. Refer to section 8.1.8.</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Device Self-test Supported (DSTS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Device Self-test command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Device Self-test command.</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Namespace Management Supported (NMS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Namespace Management capability (refer to section 8.1.15). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Namespace Management capability.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Firmware Download Supported (FWDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Firmware Commit and Firmware Image Download commands. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Firmware Commit and Firmware Image Download commands.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Format NVM Supported (FNVMS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Format NVM command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Format NVM command.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Security Send Receive Supported (SSRS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Security Send and Security Receive commands. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Security Send and Security Receive commands.</td></tr><tr><td>258</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Abort Command Limit (ACL): This field indicates the maximum number of concurrently executing Abort commands (refer to section 5.1) on the Admin Queue supported by the controller. This is a 0&#x27;s based value. It is recommended that implementations support concurrent execution of a minimum of four Abort commands.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>259</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>M3</td><td>Asynchronous Event Request Limit (AERL): This field is used to convey the maximum number of concurrently outstanding Asynchronous Event Request commands supported by the controller (refer to section 5.1.2). This is a 0&#x27;s based value. It is recommended that implementations support a minimum of four Asynchronous Event Request commands outstanding simultaneously.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">260</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">R</td><td>Firmware Updates (FRMW): This field indicates capabilities regarding firmware updates. Refer to section 3.11 for more information on the firmware update process.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:6</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>Support Multiple Update Detection (SMUD): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to detect overlapping firmware/boot partition image update command sequences (refer to section 3.11 and section 8.1.3.2). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller is not able to detect overlapping firmware/boot partition image update command sequences.</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Firmware Activation Without Reset (FAWR): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports firmware activation without a reset. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller requires a reset for firmware to be activated.</td></tr><tr><td>3:1</td><td>Number Of Firmware Slots (NOFS): This field indicates the number of firmware slots supported by the domain that contains this controller. This field shall specify a value from one to seven, indicating that at least one firmware slot is supported and up to seven maximum. This corresponds to firmware slots 1 through 7</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>First Firmware Slot Read Only (FFSRO): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the first firmware slot (i.e., slot 1) is read only. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the first firmware slot (i.e., slot 1) is read/write. Implementations may choose to have a baseline read only firmware image.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="10">261</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td>Log Page Attributes (LPA): This field indicates optional attributes for log pages that are accessed via the Get Log Page command.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>Data Area 4 Support (DA4S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Data Area 4 for the Telemetry Host-Initiated and Telemetry Controller-Initiated log. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Data Area 4 for the Telemetry Host-Initiated and Telemetry Controller-Initiated log pages.</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>Miscellaneous Log Page Support (MLPS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports: 
• the Supported Log Pages log page (Log Page Identifier 0h); 
• returning the scope of each command in the Commands Supported and Effects log page (Log Page Identifier 05h); 
• the Feature Identifiers Supported and Effects log page (Log Page Identifier 12h); and 
• the NVMe-MI Commands Supported and Effects log page (Log Page Identifier 13h). 
If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller: 
• does not support returning the scope of each command in the Commands Supported and Effects log page; 
• may support the Supported Log Pages log page; 
• may support the Feature Identifiers Supported and Effects log page; and 
• may support the NVMe-MI Commands Supported and Effects log page.</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Persistent Event Support (PES): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Persistent Event log. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Persistent Event log page.</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Telemetry Support (TS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Telemetry Host-Initiated and Telemetry Controller-Initiated log pages and sending Telemetry Log Notices. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Telemetry Host-Initiated and Telemetry Controller-Initiated log pages and Telemetry Log Notice events.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Log Page Extended Data Support (LPEDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports extended data for the Get Log Page command (including extended Number of Dwords and Log Page Offset fields). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support extended data for the Get Log Page command.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Commands Supported and Effects Support (CSES): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Commands Supported and Effects log page. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Commands Supported and Effects log page.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>SMART Support (SMARTS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the SMART / Health Information log page on a per namespace basis. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the SMART / Health Information log page on a per namespace basis.</td></tr><tr><td>262</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>Error Log Page Entries (ELPE): This field indicates the maximum number of Error Information Log Entries that are stored by the controller. This field is a 0&#x27;s based value.</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>263</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td>Number of Power States Support (NPSS): This field indicates the number of NVM Express power states supported by the controller. This is a 0&#x27;s based value. Refer to section 8.1.17. Power states are numbered sequentially starting at power state 0. A controller shall support at least one power state (i.e., power state 0) and may support up to 31 additional power states (i.e., up to 32 total).</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">264</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td>Admin Vendor Specific Command Configuration (AVSCC): This field indicates the configuration settings for vendor specific Admin command handling. Refer to section 8.1.26.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:1</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Vendor Specific Command Format (VSCF): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then all vendor specific Admin commands use the format defined in Figure 93. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the format of all vendor specific Admin commands is vendor specific.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">265</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td>Autonomous Power State Transition Attributes (APSTA): This field indicates the attributes of the autonomous power state transition feature. Refer to section 8.1.17.2.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:1</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Autonomous Power Transition Support (APTS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports autonomous power state transitions. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support autonomous power state transitions.</td></tr><tr><td>267:266</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td>Warning Composite Temperature Threshold (WCTEMP): This field indicates the minimum Composite Temperature field value (reported in the SMART / Health Information log page in Figure 206) that indicates an overheating condition during which controller operation continues. Immediate remediation is recommended (e.g., additional cooling or workload reduction). The platform should strive to maintain a composite temperature less than this value.A value of 0h in this field indicates that no warning temperature threshold value is reported by the controller. Implementations compliant with NVM Express Base Specification, Revision 1.2 or later shall report a non-zero value in this field.It is recommended that implementations report a value of 0157h in this field.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>269:268</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td>Critical Composite Temperature Threshold (CCTEMP): This field indicates the minimum Composite Temperature field value (reported in the SMART / Health Information log page in Figure 206) that indicates a critical overheating condition (e.g., may prevent continued normal operation, possibility of data loss, automatic device shutdown, extreme performance throttling, or permanent damage).A value of 0h in this field indicates that no critical temperature threshold value is reported by the controller. Implementations compliant with NVM Express Base Specification, Revision 1.2 or later shall report a non-zero value in this field.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>271:270</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Maximum Time for Firmware Activation (MTFA): This field indicates the maximum time the controller temporarily stops processing commands to activate the firmware image. This field shall be valid if the controller supports firmware activation without a reset. This field is specified in 100 millisecond units. A value of 0h indicates that the maximum time is undefined.For the amount of time to process a Firmware Commit command that specifies a value of 011b in the Commit Action field (i.e., firmware activation without reset), refer to the Maximum Processing Time for Firmware Activation Without Reset field.</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>275:272</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Host Memory Buffer Preferred Size (HMPRE): This field indicates the preferred size that the host is requested to allocate for the Host Memory Buffer feature in 4 KiB units. This value shall be greater than or equal to the Host Memory Buffer Minimum Size. If this field is non-zero, then the Host Memory Buffer feature is supported. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the Host Memory Buffer feature is not supported.If the Host Managed Live Migration Support (HMLMS) bit is set to ‘1’ in one or more controllers in the NVM subsystem; then this field shall be cleared to 0h.</td></tr><tr><td>279:276</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Host Memory Buffer Minimum Size (HMMIN): This field indicates the minimum size that the host is requested to allocate for the Host Memory Buffer feature in 4 KiB units. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the host is requested to allocate any amount of host memory possible up to the HMPRE value.</td></tr><tr><td>295:280</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Total NVM Capacity (TNVMCAP): This field indicates the total NVM capacity that is accessible by the controller. The value is in bytes. This field shall be supported if the Namespace Management capability (refer to section 8.1.15) is supported or if the Capacity Management capability (refer to section 8.1.4) is supported.Refer to section 3.8.</td></tr><tr><td>311:296</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Unallocated NVM Capacity (UNVMCAP): This field indicates the unallocated NVM capacity that is accessible by the controller. The value is in bytes. This field shall be supported if the Namespace Management capability (refer to section 8.1.15) is supported or if the Capacity Management capability (refer to section 8.1.4) is supported.Refer to section 3.8.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">315:312</td><td rowspan="7">O</td><td rowspan="7">O</td><td rowspan="7">R</td><td>Replay Protected Memory Block Support (RPMBS): This field indicates if the controller supports one or more Replay Protected Memory Blocks (RPMBs) and the capabilities. Refer to section 8.1.21.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits Description</td></tr><tr><td>31:24 Access Size (ASZE): If the Number of RPMB Units field is non-zero, then this field indicates the maximum number of 512B units of data that may be read or written per RPMB access by Security Send or Security Receive commands for the controller. This is a 0&#x27;s based value. A value of 0h indicates support for one unit of 512B of data.If the Number of RPMB Units field is 0h, then this field should be ignored by the host.</td></tr><tr><td>23:16 Total Size (TSZE): If the Number of RPMB Units field is non-zero, then this field indicates the number of 128 KiB units of data in each RPMB supported in the controller. This is a 0&#x27;s based value. A value of 0h indicates support for one unit of 128 KiB of data.If the Number of RPMB Units field is 0h, then this field should be ignored by the host.</td></tr><tr><td>15:06 Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>05:03Authentication Method (AUTHM): This field indicates the authentication method used to access all RPMBs in the controller. The values for this field are:Value Definition000b HMAC SHA-256 (refer to RFC 6234)001b to 111b Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>02:00 Number of RPMB Units (NRPMBU): This field indicates the number of RPMB targets the controller supports. All RPMB targets supported shall have the same capabilities as defined in the RPMBS field. A value of 0h indicates the controller does not support Replay Protected Memory Blocks. If this value is non-zero, then the controller shall support the Security Send and Security Receive commands.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>317:316</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Extended Device Self-test Time (EDSTT): If the Device Self-test command is supported, then this field indicates the nominal amount of time in one minute units that the controller takes to complete an extended device self-test operation when in power state 0. If the Device Self-test command is not supported, then this field is reserved.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">318</td><td rowspan="7">O</td><td rowspan="7">O</td><td rowspan="7">R</td><td>Device Self-test Options (DSTO): This field indicates the optional Device Self-test command or operation behaviors supported by the controller or NVM subsystem.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3">1</td><td>Host-Initiated Refresh Support (HIRS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Host-Initiated Refresh capability (refer to section 8.1.11). If cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Host-Initiated Refresh capability.</td></tr><tr><td>This bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27; if the controller does not support the Device Self-test command (i.e., the Device Self-test Supported (DSTS) bit in the OACS field is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;).</td></tr><tr><td>If the controller does not support the Device Self-test command (i.e., the DSTS bit in the OACS field is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;), then This bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Single Device Self-test Operation (SDSO): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem supports only one device self-test operation in progress at a time. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the NVM subsystem supports one device self-test operation per controller at a time.</td></tr><tr><td>319</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td>Firmware Update Granularity (FWUG): This field indicates the granularity and alignment requirement of the firmware image being updated by the Firmware Image Download command (refer to section 5.1.9). If the values specified in the NUMD field or the OFST field in the Firmware Image Download command do not conform to this granularity and alignment requirement, then the firmware update may abort with a status code of Invalid Field in Command. For the broadest interoperability with host software, it is recommended that the controller set this value to the lowest value possible. The value is reported in 4 KiB units (e.g., 1h corresponds to 4 KiB, 2h corresponds to 8 KiB). A value of 0h indicates that no information on granularity is provided. A value of FFh indicates there is no restriction (i.e., any granularity and alignment in dwords is allowed).</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>321:320</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>O</td><td>Keep Alive Support (KAS): This field indicates the granularity of the KATO field in 100 millisecond units (refer to section 5.1.25.1.8). If this field is cleared to 0h, then the Keep Alive Timer feature is not supported. The Keep Alive Timer feature is used by the Keep Alive capability as described in section 3.9.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">323:322</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td>Host Controlled Thermal Management Attributes (HCTMA): This field indicates the attributes of the host controlled thermal management feature. Refer to section 8.1.17.5.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>15:1</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Host Controlled Thermal Management Support (HCTMS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports host controlled thermal management. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support host controlled thermal management. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller shall support the Set Features command and Get Features command with the Feature Identifier field set to 10h.</td></tr><tr><td>325:324</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Minimum Thermal Management Temperature (MNTMT): This field indicates the minimum temperature, in Kelvins, that the host may request in the Thermal Management Temperature 1 field and Thermal Management Temperature 2 field of a Set Features command with the Feature Identifier field set to 10h. A value of 0h indicates that the controller does not report this field or the host controlled thermal management feature (refer to section 8.1.17.5) is not supported.</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>327:326</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Maximum Thermal Management Temperature (MXTMT): This field indicates the maximum temperature, in Kelvins, that the host may request in the Thermal Management Temperature 1 field and Thermal Management Temperature 2 field of the Set Features command with the Feature Identifier set to 10h. A value of 0h indicates that the controller does not report this field or the host controlled thermal management feature is not supported.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="10">331:328</td><td rowspan="10">O</td><td rowspan="10">O</td><td rowspan="10">R</td><td colspan="2">Sanitize Capabilities (SANICAP): This field indicates attributes for sanitize operations. If the Sanitize command is supported, then this field shall be non-zero. If the Sanitize command is not supported, then this field shall be cleared to 0h. Refer to section 8.1.24.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="6">31:30</td><td>No-Deallocate Modifies Media After Sanitize (NODMMAS): This field indicates if media is additionally modified by the controller as part of sanitize processing that had been started by a Sanitize command with the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit set to ‘1’.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Value</td><td>Definition</td></tr><tr><td>00b</td><td>Additional media modification as part of sanitize processing is not defined. Only controllers compliant with NVM Express Base Specification, Revision 1.3 and earlier, or controllers that do not support the Sanitize command are allowed to return this value.</td></tr><tr><td>01b</td><td>Media shall not be additionally modified by the controller as part of sanitize processing.</td></tr><tr><td>10b</td><td>Media shall be additionally modified by the controller as part of sanitize processing that had been started by a Sanitize command with the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit set to ‘1’.</td></tr><tr><td>11b</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>29</td><td>No-Deallocate Inhibited (NDI): If this bit is set to ‘1’ and the No-Deallocate Response Mode bit (refer to Figure 408) is set to ‘1’, then the controller deallocate all media allocated for user data before the Restricted Processing:Idle transition occurs or the Unrestricted Processing:Idle transition occurs (refer to section 8.1.24.3), even if the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit is set to ‘1’ in a Sanitize command. 
If: 
a) this bit is set to ‘1’; 
b) the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit is set to ‘1’ in a Sanitize command, and: 
1) the No-Deallocate Response Mode bit is cleared to ‘0’; or 
2) the Sanitize Config Feature (refer to section 5.1.25.1.15) is not supported, then the controller aborts the Sanitize command with a status code of Invalid Field in Command. 
If the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit is cleared to ‘0’ in a Sanitize command, then the value of this bit has no effect on the processing of that Sanitize command or on the sanitize operation that is started by that Sanitize command. 
If this bit is cleared to ‘0’, then the controller supports the No-Deallocate After Sanitize bit in a Sanitize command.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>28:04</td><td>Reserved</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"></td><td rowspan="4"></td><td rowspan="4"></td><td rowspan="4"></td><td>03</td><td>Verification Support (VERS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Media Verification state and the Post-Verification Deallocation state. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Media Verification state and does not support the Post-Verification Deallocation state.
If the BES bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27; and the CES bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>02</td><td>Overwrite Support (OWS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Overwrite sanitize operation. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Overwrite sanitize operation.</td></tr><tr><td>01</td><td>Block Erase Support (BES): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Block Erase sanitize operation. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Block Erase sanitize operation.</td></tr><tr><td>00</td><td>Crypto Erase Support (CES): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Crypto Erase sanitize operation. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Crypto Erase sanitize operation.</td></tr><tr><td>335:332</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Host Memory Buffer Minimum Descriptor Entry Size (HMMINDS): This field indicates the minimum usable size of a Host Memory Buffer Descriptor Entry in 4 KiB units. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the controller does not indicate any limitations on the Host Memory Buffer Descriptor Entry size.</td></tr><tr><td>337:336</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Host Memory Maximum Descriptors Entries (HMMAXD): This field indicates the number of usable Host Memory Buffer Descriptor Entries. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the controller does not indicate a maximum number of Host Memory Buffer Descriptor Entries.</td></tr><tr><td>339:338</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">NVM Set Identifier Maximum (NSETIDMAX): This field defines the maximum value of a valid NVM Set Identifier for any controller in the NVM subsystem. The number of NVM Sets supported by the NVM subsystem is less than or equal to NSETIDMAX.</td></tr><tr><td>341:340</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Endurance Group Identifier Maximum (ENDGIDMAX): This field defines the maximum value of a valid Endurance Group Identifier for any controller in the NVM subsystem. The number of Endurance Groups supported by the NVM subsystem is less than or equal to ENDGIDMAX.</td></tr><tr><td>342</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">ANA Transition Time (ANATT): This field indicates the maximum amount of time, in seconds, for a transition between ANA states or the maximum amount of time, in seconds, that the controller reports the ANA change state. If the controller supports Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting (refer to the CMIC field in Figure 312), then this field shall be set to a non-zero value. If the controller does not support Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting, then this field shall be cleared to 0h. Refer to section 8.1.2.4.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="10">343</td><td rowspan="10">O</td><td rowspan="10">O</td><td rowspan="10">R</td><td>Asymmetric Namespace Access Capabilities (ANACAP): This field indicates the capabilities associated with Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting (refer to section 8.1.1).</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>ANA Group ID Support (ANAGIDS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports a non-zero value in the ANAGRPID field of the Namespace Management command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support a non-zero value in the ANAGRPID field of the Namespace Management command. If the Namespace Management command is not supported, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>ANA Group ID Locked When Attached Support (ANAGIDLWAS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the ANAGRPID field in the Identify Namespace data structure (refer to the NVM Command Set Specification) does not change while the namespace is attached to any controller. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the ANAGRPID field may change while the namespace is attached to any controller. Refer to section 8.1.1.2.</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Report ANA Change State (RANACS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to report ANA Change state (refer to section 8.1.1.8). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not report ANA Change state.</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Report ANA Persistent Loss State (RANAPLS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to report ANA Persistent Loss state (refer to section 8.1.1.7). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not report ANA Persistent Loss state.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Report ANA Inaccessible State (RANAIS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to report ANA Inaccessible state (refer to section 8.1.1.6). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not report ANA Inaccessible state.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Report ANA Non-Optimized State (RANANOS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to report ANA Non-Optimized state (refer to section 8.1.1.5). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not report ANA Non-Optimized state.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Report ANA Optimized State (RANAOIS): if this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller is able to report ANA Inaccessible state (refer to section 8.1.1.4). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not report ANA Inaccessible state.</td></tr><tr><td>347:344</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>ANA Group Identifier Maximum (ANAGRPMAX): This field indicates the maximum value of a valid ANA Group Identifier for any controller in the NVM subsystem. If the controller supports Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting (refer to the CMIC field in Figure 312), then this field shall be set to a non-zero value. If the controller does not support Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting, then this field shall be cleared to 0h.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>351:348</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Number of ANA Group Identifiers (NANAGRPID): This field indicates the number of ANA groups supported by the controller. If the controller supports Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting (refer to the CMIC field in Figure 312), then this field shall be set to a non-zero value that is less than or equal to the ANAGRPMAX value. If the controller does not support Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting, then this field shall be cleared to 0h.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>355:352</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Persistent Event Log Size (PELS): This field indicates the maximum reportable size for the Persistent Event Log (Refer to section 5.1.12.1.14) in 64 KiB units. If the Persistent Event Log is not supported, then this field is reserved.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>357:356</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>Domain Identifier (DID): This field indicates the identifier of the domain (refer to section 3.2.5.3) that contains this controller. If the MDS bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then this field shall be set to a non-zero value. If the NVM subsystem does not support multiple domains (i.e., the NVM subsystem consists of a single domain), then this field shall be cleared to 0h.</td><td></td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">358</td><td rowspan="5">O</td><td rowspan="5">P</td><td rowspan="5">P</td><td colspan="2">Key Per I/O Capabilities (KPIOC): This field indicates the attributes for the Key Per I/O capability (refer to section 8.1.11).</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:2</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Key Per I/O Scope (KPIOSC): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the Key Per I/O capability applies to all namespaces in the NVM subsystem when the Key Per I/O capability is enabled. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the Key Per I/O capability does not apply to all namespaces in the NVM subsystem and is allowed to be independently enabled and disabled uniquely on each namespace within the NVM subsystem. If the KPIOS bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then this bit should be ignored by the host.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Key Per I/O Supported (KPIOS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Key Per I/O capability. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Key Per I/O capability.</td></tr><tr><td>359</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>361:360</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Maximum Processing Time for Firmware Activation Without Reset (MPTFAWR): This field shall indicate the estimated maximum time in 100 ms units required by the controller to process a Firmware Commit command that specifies a value of 011b in the Commit Action field (i.e., firmware activation without reset).This field applies to Firmware Commit commands received on an NVM Express controller Admin Submission Queue or received out-of-band on a Management Endpoint (refer to the NVM Express Management Interface Specification).If firmware activation without reset is not supported, then this field shall be cleared to 0h. A value of 0h indicates that no time is indicated. This field shall not be cleared to 0h on implementations that support firmware activation without reset and that are compliant with revision 2.1 or later of this specification.</td></tr><tr><td>367:362</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>383:368</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Max Endurance Group Capacity (MEGCAP): This field indicates the maximum capacity of a single Endurance Group. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the NVM subsystem does not report a maximum Endurance Group Capacity value.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">384</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td rowspan="4">O</td><td colspan="2">Temperature Threshold Hysteresis Attributes (TMPTHHA): This field indicates attributes related to temperature threshold hysteresis. Refer to section 5.1.25.1.3.1.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:3</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>2:0</td><td>Temperature Threshold Maximum Hysteresis (TMPTHMH): This field indicates the maximum temperature hysteresis value in Kelvins that is supported by the controller. This is the absolute value of the difference.If the controller does not support this attribute, this field shall be cleared to 000b.</td></tr><tr><td>385</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>387:386</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td colspan="2">Command Quiesce Time (CQT): This field indicates the expected worst-case time in 1 millisecond units for the controller to quiesce all outstanding commands (i.e., the controller shall satisfy all Immediate Abort requirements for those commands (refer to section 5.1.1.1)) after a Keep Alive Timeout (refer to section 3.9) or other communication loss (refer to section 9.6). If this field is cleared to 0h, then a command quiesce time is not reported. If the controller does not require any time to quiesce, the controller should set this field to 1h (i.e., 1 millisecond).</td></tr><tr><td>511:388</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td colspan="2">Reserved</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td colspan="6">NVM Command Set Attributes</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">512</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td colspan="2">Submission Queue Entry Size (SQES): This field defines the required and maximum I/O Submission Queue entry size.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:4</td><td>Maximum I/O Submission Queue Entry Size (MAXSQES): This field identifies the maximum I/O Submission Queue entry size when using the NVM Command Set. This value is greater than or equal to the required SQ entry size (i.e., the MIOSQES field). The value is in bytes and is reported as a power of two (2^n). The recommended value is 6, corresponding to a standard SQ entry size of 64 bytes. Controllers that implement proprietary extensions may support a larger value.</td></tr><tr><td>3:0</td><td>Minimum I/O Submission Queue Entry Size (MINSQES): This field identifies the required (i.e., minimum) I/O Submission Queue entry size. This is the minimum entry size that may be used. The value is in bytes and is reported as a power of two (2^n). The required value shall be 6, corresponding to 64.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">513</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td colspan="2">Completion Queue Entry Size (CQES): This field defines the required and maximum I/O Completion Queue entry size.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:4</td><td>Maximum I/O Completion Queue Entry Size (MAXCQES): This field identifies the maximum I/O Completion Queue entry size. This value is greater than or equal to the required CQ entry size (i.e., the MIOCQES field). The value is in bytes and is reported as a power of two (2^n). The recommended value is 4, corresponding to a standard CQ entry size of 16 bytes. Controllers that implement proprietary extensions may support a larger value.</td></tr><tr><td>3:0</td><td>Minimum I/O Completion Queue Entry Size (MINCQES): This field identifies the required (i.e., minimum) I/O Completion Queue entry size. This is the minimum entry size that may be used. The value is in bytes and is reported as a power of two (2^n). The required value shall be 4, corresponding to 16.</td></tr><tr><td>515:514</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td colspan="2">Maximum Outstanding Commands (MAXCMD): Indicates the maximum number of commands that the controller processes at one time for a particular queue (which may be larger than the size of the corresponding Submission Queue). The host may use this value to size Completion Queues and optimize the number of commands submitted at one time to a particular I/O Queue. This field is mandatory for NVMe over Fabrics implementations and optional for NVMe over PCIe implementations. If the field is not used, it shall be cleared to 0h.</td></tr><tr><td>519:516</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Number of Namespaces (NN): This field indicates the maximum value of a valid NSID for the NVM subsystem. Refer to the MNAN field for the number of supported namespaces in the NVM subsystem.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="10">521:520</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">R</td><td>Optional NVM Command Support (ONCS): This field indicates the optional I/O commands and features supported by the controller. Refer to section 3.1.3.If a controller supports more than one I/O Command Set, then the bits in the field associated with command support reflect the aggregate support across all of the I/O Command Sets supported or I/O Command Sets inherited from I/O Command Sets (e.g., Zoned Namespace Command Set). The Commands Supported and Effects log page (refer to section 5.1.12.1.6) may be used to determine the support of commands for a specific I/O Command Set.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>15:13</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>12</td><td>Namespace Zeroes Support (NSZS): If this bit is set to '1', then the controller supports the Namespace Zeroes (NSZ) bit in the NVM Command Set Write Zeroes command. If this bit is cleared to '0', then the controller does not support the Namespace Zeroes (NSZ) bit in the Write Zeroes command. If the Write Zeroes command is not supported, then this bit shall be cleared to '0'</td></tr><tr><td>11</td><td>Maximum Write Zeroes with Deallocate (MAXWZD): If this bit is set to '1', then the maximum data size for the NVM Command Set Write Zeroes command depends on the value of the Deallocate bit in the Write Zeroes command and the value in the WZDSL field in the I/O Command Set specific Identify Controller data structure for the NVM Command Set (refer to the I/O Command Set specific Identify Controller Data Structure (CNS 06h, CSI 00h) section in the NVM Command Set Specification). If this bit is cleared to '0', then this bit has no effect. If the Write Zeroes command is not supported or the WZSL field in that data structure is cleared to 0h, then this bit shall be cleared to '0'.</td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td>NVM All Fast Copy (NVMAFC): If this bit is set to '1', then within this NVM subsystem, all copy operations performed by the controller are fast copy operations (refer to the Fast copy operations section of the NVM Command Set Specification).If this bit is cleared to '0', then within this NVM subsystem, some copy operations performed by the controller may not be fast copy operations.</td></tr><tr><td>9</td><td>NVM Copy Single Atomicity (NVMCSA): If this bit is set to '1', then the write portion of any NVM Command Set Copy command is performed as a single write command to which the atomicity requirements described in the Atomic operation section of the NVM Command Set Specification apply. If this bit is cleared to '0', then the atomicity behavior of the write portion of Copy commands is specified by the Copy atomicity section of the NVM Command Set Specification.This bit should be set to '1' if the controller supports the NVM Command Set Copy command. The Copy atomicity section of the NVM Command Set Specification specifies additional conditions under which this bit shall be set to '1'.This bit is ignored by the host if the controller does not support the NVM Command Set Copy command.</td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td>Copy Support (NVMCPS): If this bit is set to '1', then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Copy command. If this bit is cleared to '0', then the controller does not support the NVM Command Set Copy command.</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>Verify Support (NVMFYS): If this bit is set to '1', then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Verify command and the Verify Size Limit (VSL) field indicates the recommended maximum data size for Verify commands. If this bit is cleared to '0', then controller support of the NVM Command Set Verify command is indicated by a non-zero data size limit in the VSL field.</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>Timestamp Support (TSS): If this bit is set to '1', then the controller supports the Timestamp feature. If this bit is cleared to '0', then the controller does not support the Timestamp feature. Refer to section 5.1.25.1.7.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7"></td><td rowspan="7"></td><td rowspan="7"></td><td rowspan="7"></td><td>5</td><td>Reservations Support (RESERVES): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports reservations. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support reservations. If the controller supports reservations, then the following commands associated with reservations shall be supported: Reservation Report, Reservation Register, Reservation Acquire, and Reservation Release. Refer to section 8.1.22 for additional requirements.</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Save and Select Feature Support (SSFS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Save field set to a non-zero value in the Set Features command and the Select field set to a non-zero value in the Get Features command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Save field set to a non-zero value in the Set Features command and does not support the Select field set to a non-zero value in the Get Features command.</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Write Zeroes Support Variants (NVMWZSV): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Write Zeroes command and the Write Zeroes Size Limit (WZSL) field indicates the recommended maximum data size for Write Zeroes commands. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then controller support of the NVM Command Set Write Zeroes command is indicated by a non-zero data size limit in the WZSL field.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Dataset Management Support Variants (NVMDSMSV): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Dataset Management command and limits, if any, on controller support of the Dataset Management command are indicated by non-zero values in the Dataset Management Ranges Limit (DMRL) field, the Dataset Management Size Limit (DMSL) field and the Dataset Management Range Size Limit (DMRSL) field. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then controller support of the NVM Command Set Dataset Management command is indicated by a non-zero data size limit in the DMRL, DMSL, and DMRSL fields.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Write Uncorrectable Support Variants (NVMWUSV): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Write Uncorrectable command and the Write Uncorrectable Size Limit (WUSL) field indicates the recommended maximum data size for Write Uncorrectable commands. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then controller support of the NVM Command Set Write Uncorrectable command is indicated by a non-zero data size limit in the WUSL field.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Compare Command Support (NVMCMPS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Compare command. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the NVM Command Set Compare command.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2">NOTE: This field applies to all I/O Command Sets. The original name has been retained for historical continuity.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">523:522</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td colspan="2">Fused Operation Support (FUSES): This field indicates the fused operations that the controller supports. Refer to section 3.4.2.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:1</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Fused Compare and Write Supported (FCWS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the NVM Command Set Compare and Write fused operation. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the NVM Command Set Compare and Write fused operation. Compare shall be the first command in the sequence.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">524</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">M</td><td rowspan="7">R</td><td>Format NVM Attributes (FNA): This field indicates attributes for the Format NVM command.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:4</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Format NVM Broadcast Support (FNVMBS): This bit indicates whether the Format NVM command supports an NSID value set to FFFFFFFH. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the Format NVM command does not support an NSID value set to FFFFFFFH. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the Format NVM command supports an NSID value set to FFFFFFFH.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Cryptographic Erase Supported (CRYES): This bit indicates whether cryptographic erase is supported as part of the secure erase functionality. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then cryptographic erase is supported. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then cryptographic erase is not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Secure Erase Namespace Scope (SENS): This bit indicates whether secure erase functionality applies to all namespaces in the NVM subsystem or is specific to a particular namespace. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then any secure erase performed as part of a format operation results in a secure erase of all namespaces in the NVM subsystem. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then any secure erase performed as part of a format results in a secure erase of the particular specified namespace. If the FNVMBS bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Format Namespace Scope (FNS): This bit indicates whether the format operation (excluding secure erase) applies to all namespaces in the NVM subsystem or is specific to a particular namespace. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then all namespaces in the NVM subsystem shall be configured with the same attributes and a format (excluding secure erase) of any namespace results in a format of all namespaces in the NVM subsystem. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller supports format on a per namespace basis. If the FNVMBS bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td colspan="2">Description</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="10">525</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">M</td><td rowspan="10">R</td><td colspan="2">Volatile Write Cache (VWC): This field indicates attributes related to the presence of a volatile write cache in the controller.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>07:03</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>02:01</td><td>Flush Behavior (FB): This field indicates Flush command behavior (refer to section 7.2) if the NSID value is set to FFFFFFFh as follows:</td></tr><tr><td>Value</td><td>Definition</td></tr><tr><td>00b</td><td>Support for the NSID field set to FFFFFFFh is not indicated. Only controllers compliant with NVM Express Base Specification revision 1.3 and earlier shall be allowed to return this value.</td></tr><tr><td>01b</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>10b</td><td>The Flush command does not support the NSID field set to FFFFFFFh. The controller shall fail a Flush command with the NSID set to FFFFFFFh with a status code of Invalid Namespace or Format.</td></tr><tr><td>11b</td><td>The Flush command supports the NSID field set to FFFFFFFh.</td></tr><tr><td>00</td><td>Volatile Write Cache Present (VWCP): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then a volatile write cache is present in the controller. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then a volatile write cache is not present in the controller. If a volatile write cache is present, then the host controls whether the volatile write cache is enabled with a Set Features command specifying the Volatile Write Cache feature identifier (refer to section 5.1.25.1.4). The Flush command (refer to section 7.2) is used to request that the contents of a volatile write cache be made non-volatile.</td></tr><tr><td>527:526</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Atomic Write Unit Normal (AWUN): This field is specific to namespaces that are associated with command sets that specify logical blocks (i.e., Command Set Identifier 0h or 2h), and shall be cleared to 0h for namespaces that are not associated with command sets that specify logical blocks. Refer to the applicable I/O Command Set specification (e.g., the Atomic Operation section of the NVM Command Set Specification).</td></tr><tr><td>529:528</td><td>M</td><td>M</td><td>R</td><td colspan="2">Atomic Write Unit Power Fail (AWUPF): This field is specific to namespaces that are associated with command sets that specify logical blocks (i.e., Command Set Identifier 0h or 2h), and shall be cleared to 0h for namespaces that are not associated with command sets that specify logical blocks. Refer to the applicable I/O Command Set specification (e.g., the Atomic Operation section of the NVM Command Set Specification).</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">530</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">M</td><td rowspan="4">R</td><td colspan="2">I/O Command Set Vendor Specific Command Configuration (ICSVSCC): This field indicates the configuration settings for vendor specific I/O command handling. Refer to section 8.1.26.</td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:1</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Same NVM Vendor Specific Command Format (SNVSCF): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then all vendor specific I/O commands use the format defined in Figure 93. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the format of all vendor specific I/O commands is vendor specific.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="6">531</td><td rowspan="6">M</td><td rowspan="6">M</td><td rowspan="6">R</td><td>Namespace Write Protection Capabilities (NWPC): This field indicates the optional namespace write protection capabilities supported by the controller. Refer to section 8.1.16.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>7:3</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Permanent Write Protect Support (PWPS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Permanent Write Protect state. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Permanent Write Protect state. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller shall support the Write Protection Control field (refer to section 8.1.16.1).If the NWPWPS bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Write Protect Until Power Cycle Support (WPUPCS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Write Protect Until Power Cycle state. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Write Protect Until Power Cycle state. If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller shall support the Write Protection Control field (refer to section 8.1.16.1).If the NWPWPS bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then this bit shall be cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>No Write Protect and Write Protect Support (NWPWPS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller shall support the No Write Protect and Write Protect namespace write protection states and may support the Write Protect Until Power Cycle state and Permanent Write Protect namespace write protection states (refer to section 8.1.16). If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Namespace Write Protection.</td></tr><tr><td>533:532</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td>Atomic Compare &amp; Write Unit (ACWU): This field is specific to namespaces that are associated with command sets that specify logical blocks (i.e., Command Set Identifier 0h or 2h), and shall be cleared to 0h for namespaces that are not associated with command sets that specify logical blocks. Refer to the applicable I/O Command Set specification (e.g., the Atomic Operation section of the NVM Command Set Specification).</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="8">535:534</td><td rowspan="8">M</td><td rowspan="8">R</td><td rowspan="8">R</td><td>Copy Descriptor Formats Supported (CDFS):</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>15:5</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Copy Descriptor Format 4 Support (CDF4S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Copy Descriptor Format 4h. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Copy Descriptor Format 4h.</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Copy Descriptor Format 3 Support (CDF3S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Copy Descriptor Format 3h. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Copy Descriptor Format 3h.</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Copy Descriptor Format 2 Support (CDF2S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Copy Descriptor Format 2h. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Copy Descriptor Format 2h.</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Copy Descriptor Format 1 Support (CDF1S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Copy Descriptor Format 1h. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Copy Descriptor Format 1h.</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Copy Descriptor Format 0 Support (CDF0S): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports Copy Descriptor Format 0h. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support Copy Descriptor Format 0h.</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td><td></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="18">539:536</td><td rowspan="18">O</td><td rowspan="18">O</td><td rowspan="18">M</td><td>SGL Support (SGLS): This field indicates if SGLs are supported and the particular SGL types supported. Refer to section 4.3.2.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Bits</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>31:22</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>21</td><td>Transport SGL Data Block Descriptor Support (TSDBDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Transport SGL Data Block descriptor. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Transport SGL Data Block descriptor.</td></tr><tr><td>20</td><td>SGL Address Offset Supported (SAOS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Address field in SGL Data Block, SGL Segment, and SGL Last Segment descriptor types specifying an offset. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the Address field specifying an offset is not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>19</td><td>MPTR SGL Descriptor Support (MSDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then use of a Metadata Pointer (MPTR) that contains an address of an SGL segment containing exactly one SGL Descriptor that is qword aligned is supported. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then use of a MPTR containing an SGL Descriptor is not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>18</td><td>Length Larger than Data Transfer Support (LLDTS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports commands that contain a data or metadata SGL of a length larger than the amount of data to be transferred. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the SGL length shall be equal to the amount of data to be transferred.</td></tr><tr><td>17</td><td>Metadata Buffer Alignment (MBA): This bit indicates metadata buffer alignment requirements when CDW0.PSDT is set to 01b (refer to Figure 91). If set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then use of a byte aligned contiguous physical buffer of metadata (the Metadata Pointer field in Figure 92) is supported. If cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then use of a byte aligned contiguous physical buffer of metadata is not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>16</td><td>SGL Bit Bucket Descriptor Supported (SBBDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the SGL Bit Bucket descriptor is supported. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the SGL Bit Bucket descriptor is not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>15:08</td><td>SGL Descriptor Threshold (SDT): This field indicates the recommended maximum number of SGL descriptors in a command (refer to section 4.3.2). If this field is cleared to 0h, then no recommended maximum number of SGL descriptors is reported.</td></tr><tr><td>07:03</td><td>Reserved</td></tr><tr><td>02</td><td>Keyed SGL Data Block Descriptor Support (KSDBDS): If this bit is set to &#x27;1&#x27;, then the controller supports the Keyed SGL Data Block descriptor. If this bit is cleared to &#x27;0&#x27;, then the controller does not support the Keyed SGL Data Block descriptor.</td></tr><tr><td>01:00</td><td>SGL Support (SGLS): This field is used to determine the SGL support. Valid values are shown in the table below.</td></tr><tr><td>Value</td><td>Definition</td></tr><tr><td>00b</td><td>SGLs are not supported.</td></tr><tr><td>01b</td><td>SGLs are supported. There is no alignment nor granularity requirement for Data Blocks.</td></tr><tr><td>10b</td><td>SGLs are supported. There is a dword alignment and granularity requirement for Data Blocks (refer to section 4.3.2).</td></tr><tr><td>11b</td><td>Reserved</td></tr></table>


Figure 312: Identify – Identify Controller Data Structure, I/O Command Set Independent


<table><tr><td>Bytes</td><td>I/O1</td><td>Admin1</td><td>Disc1</td><td>Description</td></tr><tr><td>543:540</td><td>O</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>Maximum Number of Allowed Namespaces (MNAN): This field indicates the maximum number of namespaces supported by the NVM subsystem. If this field is cleared to 0h, then the maximum number of namespaces supported by the NVM subsystem is less than or equal to the value in the NN field. If the controller supports Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting, then this field shall be set to a non-zero value that is less than or equal to the NN value.</td></tr><tr><td>559:544</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td>Maximum Domain Namespace Attachments (MAXDNA): Indicates the maximum of the sum of the number of namespaces attached to each I/O controller in the Domain. If this field is cleared to 0h, then no maximum is specified.The value of this field shall be the same value for all I/O controllers in the Domain.</td></tr><tr><td>563:560</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td>Maximum I/O Controller Namespace Attachments (MAXCNA): Indicates the maximum number of namespaces that are allowed to be attached to this I/O controller. If this field is cleared to 0h, then no maximum is specified.The value of this field shall be less than or equal to the number of namespaces supported by the NVM subsystem (refer to the MNAN field).</td></tr><tr><td>567:564</td><td>O</td><td>R</td><td>R</td><td>Optimal Aggregated Queue Depth (OAQD): Indicates the recommended maximum total number of outstanding I/O commands across all I/O queues on the controller for optimal operation. The host may use this value to limit the number of commands outstanding at one time across all I/O queues on the controller.If this field is cleared to 0h, then the Optimal Aggregated Queue Depth is
